steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim has two parts: Joanna admires ambition in others, and she does so to the exclusion of all other qualities. The winning quotation must show both — she values purposeful people, and she is indifferent or dismissive toward everyone else. A quotation that only shows her traits, her family preference, or her valuing multiple qualities fails.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — '"She had a good sense of logic, a strong power of concentration, and a remarkably retentive and visualizing memory."': Describes Joanna's own intellectual qualities, not what she admires in others. Irrelevant to the claim. ✗
  • Choice B — '"Indeed from the very beginning Joanna showed her preference for her father."': Shows a family preference with no explanation. Nothing here connects to admiring ambition, let alone to the exclusion of other qualities. ✗
  • Choice C — '"Joanna was mightily interested in people who had a ‘purpose’ in life. Otherwise not at all."': "Purpose" represents ambition, and "Otherwise not at all" is the exclusion clause in action — she is completely uninterested in everyone who lacks it. Both parts of the claim are satisfied. ✓
  • Choice D — '"Joanna was like her father not only so far as ambition was concerned but also in her willingness to work."': Mentions ambition but adds a second quality she values (work ethic). This contradicts "to the exclusion of all other qualities" rather than illustrating it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
